methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $231M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: SambaNova Systems
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series D), post-money $5B

private.name: Tenstorrent
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series D), post-money $2.7B

data.narrative:
Pragmatic Semiconductor is a Series D chip design company that has raised $231M to date. The valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of 8 private companies and 3 public semiconductor comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD), with direct peers including SambaNova Systems, Tenstorrent, d-Matrix, and DEEPX across Series C and D stages.

Two triangulation methods apply here. Stage-progression analysis uses the $231M total raised as a baseline proxy and applies a 1.5x step-up multiplier typical of Series D-to-next-stage progression, yielding $346M. Peer-median anchoring is more directly applicable: four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables carry a median valuation of $2.4B, with a range spanning $529M (DEEPX, Series C) to $5.0B (SambaNova, Series D). Public-comp P/S anchoring cannot be applied without the target's disclosed revenue figures. The median valuation across the two applicable methods is $1.3B, with a low estimate of $295M and a high of $2.7B.

The wide $295M–$2.7B range reflects genuine uncertainty around Pragmatic's revenue traction, margins, and competitive position relative to the peer set. The peer-median method carries more weight given the direct stage and sector alignment; however, the substantial dispersion among peers themselves ($529M to $5.0B) suggests that company-specific factors—technology differentiation, customer concentration, and path to profitability—are material valuation drivers not captured by stage alone.
